summary: The post draws a parallel between two seemingly unrelated phenomena: rising college costs and Monsanto's big data strategy for agriculture. Both are characterized as arms races that benefit the powerful institutions while harming individuals (families and farmers respectively). O'Neil argues that the only solution to an arms race is an armistice, but such agreements won't happen when one party profits from the escalation. Colleges benefit from tuition competition while parents suffer, and Monsanto deliberately creates competitive pressure to force independent farmers into their system.
key_points: College costs are rising due to a perverse combination of US News rankings gaming, federal loan incentives, and reduced state funding
Arms races can only be solved through armistice, but armistices won't happen when powerful players benefit from the race
Monsanto already has farmers' data through surveillance regardless of whether farmers opt into their analytics services
Monsanto is deliberately creating an arms race to force non-Monsanto farmers into economic dependence on their system
The asymmetry of power means individual actors (families, farmers) cannot escape these systems without collective action
money_quotes: When you have an arms race, the only solution is an armistice.
An armistice won't happen if the players in question are benefitting from the arms race.
Monsanto already has all your data, and will have it forever, due to their surveillance.
They are explicitly creating an arms race between Monsanto farmers and non-Monsanto farmers.

If they give Monsanto farmers an extra boost, it will lead other farmers into the conclusion that, without such a boost, they won't be able to keep up, and they will be forced into the Monsanto system by economic necessity.
key_insight: Powerful institutions deliberately engineer arms races that force individuals into dependency, and these races persist because the institutions profit from the escalation while individuals bear the costs.
